speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. And the survivor is Mr. Victor Dortheimer. Before the tape break, you were saying how when you arrived at the ghetto that you didn't know where some people were being taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we finished this, again. No?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I would like to know what was it like in the ghetto? What were the conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall we start? Well, when we came to the ghetto, it was '42, June. This ghetto was full up, because normal was for 10,000 or 12,000-- I don't know. But suddenly, it was three times, four times so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we haven't got even where to go. But the-- the people in ghetto, they suddenly in one room was five, six, seven people.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been lucky because it was family with four people. And they took me and Helena, my wife, to them, because they were too big flat. Because was one room and kitchen. So we were, all together six people there. But after they took I don't know exactly how many thousand people to-- to send-- they sent to the to Belzec, this was little bit better. Because 10,000 or 12,000, they sent to their death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So suddenly, the ghetto was half empty. And this is going on until October, '42, where there's another action and another few thousand, I don't know-- how many thousand people they sent again to the gas chamber. And in the moment, the ghetto was almost empty. So they cut off half ghetto for Polish people. And the rest, we were Jews, just in small ghetto. And this was '42, October. And suddenly, it was rumored that the Germans starting to build on the Jewish cemetery, barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we find out this is for us, for these people from ghetto, what just-- to-- to be-- to stay in this ghetto. And they were working, and they're building until March '43. And March '43, 13 of March, the ghetto was surrounded. And we-- there was a big panic, because the ghetto was divide in ghetto B and ghetto A. And it was divide with-- with wires, and police-- Jewish police. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The ghetto A, this was working people. And ghetto B, what they don't-- didn't work this time-- old people, children, and not-working people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this 13 of March, '43, after they surrounded, they took from ghetto A, every people to the barracks on the Jewish cemetery. And they left-- they left what is-- they stay in the ghetto B.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came-- this was Saturday-- when we came to the cemetery, they give us order to dig a big huge-- I-- we didn't know-- huge grave. But we get a little suspicion why we're digging. And this was taking until morning, 4 o'clock in the morning. 9 o'clock, was a Sunday, next day, they start to bring dead people. They shot every single one was in ghetto B-- they was shot. And they brought them to this big grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we-- borrowed-- how you say? How would  you say? Buried.

speaker: interviewee
text: We buried them. It was the end of ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's-- you said this was a cemetery. What was the name of the place where you were? What was the name where you buried--

speaker: interviewee
text: This cemetery, this is area, this was called Plaszow. Plaszow. And there was just SS and barracks, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we haven't got very, very little to eat. But in, I think, after five days, the SS surrounded every barracks. And they said, everything what we got-- money, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] gold-- everything which was valuable, to get up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody was afraid. In our barracks, the-- one SS told us, if we don't give everything, we're going to be like this one. And he shot one of us, just to example of what are you going to be if we don't give them. So they took everything from us.

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- after one or two weeks, I personally was appointed decorator. I was in a group-- a group, what we got a big huge workshop with painting, decorating. It was 50 people. And we haven't got what to do. No organization. But in this camp was Jewish police. And the Jewish police, their manager was named Chilowicz, what his-- with his wife-- C-H-I-L-O-W-I-C-Z. And suddenly, we saw through the window that the Chilowicz is coming with a commander, Goeth. Because it was the SS commander. And they-- we saw that they coming to our barracks. And with-- with me, was father, son, and grandson. And the son of this father-- his father was very old master painter. And the son was also a painter. And the son said, I'm going-- I'm going to make report to the commander, because I-- I know German, he told. Well, please, everybody was happy that he was going to. And the commander came with Chilowicz together. And-- the-- the-- the-- what is-- what they wanted to-- give report to the commander, his name was Faerber. I spell you the name-- F-A-E-R-B-E-R. And he told him, commander, please, we are 50 people working. And the commander said, OK, come on, to three. I'm going to check you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we start to three. We were standing to three. And he was checking. It was-- he told him it's 50 people. And suddenly he said, excuse me, but this is 49. And he said to the commander, commander, I'm sorry. But I think that one is in toilet. And the commander took out revolver from his pocket. He said to him, kapo, what is kapo? He's shouldn't think, he must know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he shot him. And he was lying dead on the floor. And the commander said to the Chilowicz, please go and bring me four SS people with whips. And they came to us. And the commander said, everyone from these people getting, on their bottom, 10 whip, 10 lashes. And they beat us, 10 lashes. I couldn't sleep 40 days, every one of us. This was the first what I saw Commander Goeth. But fortunately, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after six weeks, suddenly my manager, what was the master of painting, decorator, older than me. He came to me, he said, you are the painter. You know German. You should take four people with you. You're going to factory as a painter. I didn't know where I'm going. It was a group. And they put me to the group. We came to factory. And so then, it came beautiful, handsome man-- a tall blonde. And he ask, where they were, the painters. And these four people said, Victor, you must talk. You speak German. I said, I am the painter. OK. And we got this and this and this. I spoke German to him. OK, you're going to be the master here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I saw the Schindler. And from that time after, we went every day to-- to come and return. And we'd been afraid to go to the camp, because every day they were killing people. So the Schindler, I don't know how he managed, that he get permission to make for the factory people, his camp near factory. And suddenly, we went to-- to camp. And we never can return, because barracks and everything was for us to make. So this was '43, May. And I was staying with Schindler until '44, October, what-- what we liquidate the factory. And this factory, because the Russians was not far away. So the Schindler, because he wants to rescue the Jews, I don't know how he manage.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know about it. I'm not going to talk about details. He went to Czechoslovakia, what was occupied from the German. And then he got permission to transfer his factory to Czechoslovakia, to Sudetenland. And of course, we-- before we-- before we liquidate the-- the Emalia, there was 700 people, with 300 people stay just for liquidate factory. Of course, I, with my wife, are between these people. And after we liquidate, we went to Plaszow again. And from Plaszow, because the Schindler transfer the factory to Sudeten, we went-- men-- to Gross Rosen, just to register us. And the women went to Auschwitz-- also the same, to register. And we met all together in Schindler factory in Sudetenland. And this was October, '44. And we had been staying there until liberation from the Russian army. This is the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I go back to the ghetto, the Krakow ghetto, can you tell me what the conditions were like? You said you slept in one room. And you said you slept with a few families. Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember the name. And maybe, if you saw the I'm going to tell you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This girl, what you remember-- he came to Schindler with false papers to beg Schindler to take her pardons, what is the name of Peltzman, Regina and Herman Peltzman. I was living with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was two children, Maria Peltzman, and a son. The son was-- not normal, you know? There was something wrong with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '42, when the German took the thousands of thousand peoples to the gas chamber, they took this boy with. And then Maria, the daughter, she was born in Buenos Aires. When the parents saw what is going on in ghetto-- because nobody was expecting they are going to kill us-- they remind her that the that daughter is born-- is born in Buenos Aires. So they wrote to embassy to Berlin, the story. And from embassy there came a letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: She should come to Berlin to take the Argentine passport. Now, how you could, a Jewish girl, go to Berlin in '43? We got death sentence.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the German, they like money. And then bribed the high-- high-ranking officer. That she got permission to go to Berlin and return. And when she came return with Argentine passport, next day, she went outside from ghetto. She took, already, a room. And this was- this was the story. This is what we went together. In this film, they say, they show you, that the Maria no false--no false paper. No, she was with Argentine paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can this stop? Because I wanted to show you this Peltzman after the war with Schindler. I got a picture. Show us later, when we do the photographs, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we survive. Yeah, I remember when we came-- we came so fast, the men, to the factory, to Sudeten. And we were expecting-- because 90% of the woman-- of the women, they are wives. It was Schindler wanted the couples to keep together. When we came, suddenly one week, another week, the women are not there. So another. We start to complain. Schindler, Schindler, OK. I'm going to manage. Schindler send secretary, Frau Hoffmann, to Auschwitz. Because there was the list. And after she went to Auschwitz, they-- the women came to us. This was the story. Now, in this factory was approximately down to 200 people, altogether. Then there came thousands. But some-- sometimes, I don't remember exactly, it was strong-- a very heavy winter for us. They came, a few wagons with prisoner. And the wagons was freezing-- frozen. They put it fire. They opened the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was maybe 80 people, 70, I don't know. 60% were dead, and 40%, they were like skeletal. You know? Skeletal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Schindler, with his wife, they start to buy black market food for them. And they survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back again to the Krakow ghetto-- I'm sorry, I keep moving back there. But what type of things did you do when you were in there? Did work when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was working local-- local, hospitals, painting and just painting. You know? Just to-- to be on the list that I'm working.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get paid?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. A soup. [LAUGHS] Listen, in ghetto, most of these people, they haven't got income. They-- they were selling everything, what was possible, to the Polish people. Because the Polish people, somehow they get permission to go on the-- I don't know the circumstances-- they get permission to go to ghetto. And they took trousers, or the jacket, or the-- because the Jews were nice dressed. They were selling everything, for bread, for nothing, you know, just to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did the food come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Smuggling. Smuggling. The police, German police-- Jewish police and Polish police, they were together. They bribed everybody. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of food was it? What type of food did you get to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please?

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of food did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? Soup, bread. WHAT was possible. I was smuggling, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you-- how did you-- who did you smuggle to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the cellar, I went out and returned. It was this-- [INAUDIBLE]. Everything was dead. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody leave the ghetto and not come back? But not being transported, did anybody try to escape from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lot of people, well, they left ghetto, and the false papers. And everyone, what the German caught, they were shot. Not another punishment, just shot. Everyone what they caught, immediately shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see-- when-- sorry, when you were transported, when you left the ghetto, how did you leave? Were you on foot? Were you put into cars-- lorries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a second. When I leave ghetto? When?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were liquidated, when the ghetto was liquidated.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you get to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: On foot. Walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is one hour, three quarter hour. It was not too far. Because the cemetery was Krakow's-- Jewish Krakow cemetery, a little bit outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We knew. We knew the barrack for us. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know what was going on in Germany, or did you know what was going on anywhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly. In Germany, was-- you know-- they didn't do anything in Germany. Everything, what the killing the Jews, and the-- another nation, it was in Poland or Russia. Not in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany there was ordinary concentration camp-- like Buchenwald, Dachau, and Ravensbruck. You know? And you mentioned that your wife, Helena, when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. I told you. We had chuppah, '41. Remember? '41, in [NON-ENGLISH]. Correct?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when onto Plaszow, did you see anybody else? When you were in Plaszow, did you see anybody else that was there, apart from the people from the ghetto, from the Krakow ghetto? Was there anyone else that was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my-- my friends? We were together in Plaszow. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Goeth like? What was he-- what did he look like? The commandant?

speaker: interviewee
text: The commander? Well, a tall man, very tall, very handsome man, slim, nice, brutal, murder. He was from Vienna, you know. But we-- when the commander went out to the-- to our camp, it was panic. Because didn't exist that he must shoot somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did he used to randomly shoot people then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he shoot people from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? [LAUGHS] For nothing. Because he didn't like. Why are you looking at me so-- you are, please, you don't like me? Shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing. It was-- and this-- listen. Remember that we got death sentence anyway. So what was the difference for him? He needs today, one or two more. He knew that he's going to kill everybody eventually. Why he did this, why he didn't kill everyone? Because he got nice job. If he's going to kill everyone, what's he got? Nothing. He's going to the front. But he was like king-- money, the women, everybody, everything. Why should he-- why should he have killed everyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got, for example, 15,000 people. One was shoemaker, what's doing shoes for the SS and for the girlfriend and for-- and for wives. Another, that were tailor, that was making uniform for them, beautiful. And chefs-- everything was in-- in the camp. Every profession was in the camp. Fantastic people. Goldsmith-- they made from gold, everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't kill us. Not because he was sorry for us. Because he wants to keep the job. And did you see his house?

speaker: interviewee
text: House? I was-- when-- we-- we were painting, from beginning, his villa. But after he went to the villa to live, I was in Emalia already, by Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his villa like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his villa like? You said you painted his villa. What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. It was an empty villa. Empty. From time to time, he came here and you're working too slow.
